scenario.title: Altitude Hold Disengage
scenario.narrative: A flight control law disengages altitude hold when vertical speed exceeds the limit and either the autopilot is engaged or the pilot has override active.

claim: This 4-test set satisfies MCDC for verticalSpeedExceedsLimit, autopilotEngaged, and pilotOverride.
charges: The flight control law disengages altitude hold only when verticalSpeed > LIMIT AND (autopilotEngaged OR pilotOverride).
The defendant submits a 4-test suite and claims MC/DC coverage for all three conditions of `verticalSpeedExceedsLimit && (autopilotEngaged || pilotOverride)`.

The prosecution alleges that under short-circuit evaluation of `autopilotEngaged || pilotOverride`, an independence pair for pilotOverride is only valid when autopilotEngaged is FALSE — and the suite must demonstrate this for each condition.
hints: For each condition, find a pair of tests where only that condition changes AND the decision flips.

For pilotOverride: notice that when autopilotEngaged is true, short-circuit evaluation makes pilotOverride irrelevant. Find pairs where autopilotEngaged is FALSE so pilotOverride actually controls the outcome.

verticalSpeedExceedsLimit: T1↔T2 (only it flips, outcome flips). autopilotEngaged: T1↔T3 (only it flips, outcome flips). pilotOverride: T3↔T4 (only it flips, outcome flips). Note: short-circuiting forces pilotOverride's pair to use autopilotEngaged=false.

seeded_faults.description: Short-circuit evaluation skips pilotOverride when autopilotEngaged is true

misconceptions.explanation_md: You tested each condition in isolation, but MCDC requires *paired* test cases in which one condition changes while all others remain fixed and the decision outcome flips.
